claim: Word-initial glyph constraints (o, c, q, s, d dominating) and word-final glyph constraints (y, n, l, r, o dominating) are not merely phonotactic but reflect a codebook structure in which word-initial glyphs encode semantic category (e.g., plant part, action, quantity) and word-final glyphs encode grammatical role (e.g., noun, verb, adjective), such that co-occurrence of specific initial+final glyph pairs is non-uniform and significantly exceeds chance across all sections.
evidence_for: Positional glyph constraints are well-established and too strong to be purely phonotactic in a 25-glyph alphabet
Top words cluster around specific initial-final pairs: daiin (dâ€¦n), chedy (câ€¦y), shedy (sâ€¦y), chol (câ€¦l), chey (câ€¦y) â€” suggesting paradigmatic substitution
If initial glyphs encode semantic class, section-specific vocabulary (herbal vs. zodiac) should show different initial-glyph frequency profiles
evidence_against: A simpler explanation is that initial/final constraints are cipher artifacts (e.g., nulls or padding) with no semantic content
The low glyph alphabet size (25) limits the number of distinct categories encodable at word boundaries
Cross-section initial-glyph frequency data is not yet available to confirm section-specific profiles
test: Build a contingency table of word-initial Ã— word-final glyph pairs across all tokens; compute chi-squared statistic for independence; then stratify by section and compute per-section initial-glyph frequency profiles, testing whether section membership predicts initial-glyph distribution using a multinomial logistic regression or permutation test.
test_metric: Chi-squared statistic for initialÃ—final glyph independence and between-section initial-glyph KL divergence
test_threshold: Confirm if chi-squared p<0.001 for non-independence of initialÃ—final pairs AND KL divergence between herbal and zodiac initial-glyph distributions exceeds 0.10 nats; deny if chi-squared p>0.05 or KL divergence <0.03 nats.

reasoning: Non-random co-occurrence of boundary glyphs combined with section-specific initial profiles would provide strong evidence for a structured codebook rather than a simple substitution cipher, fundamentally changing the decipherment strategy required.
